sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Clinically significant hypoglycaemia is present when blood glucose is low enough to cause neuroglycopenic or autonomic symptoms. In practice, symptomatic patients with BGL below about 4.0 mmol/L should be treated promptly.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Weakness, shaking, diaphoresis
- Headache
- Abnormal behaviour
- Altered conscious state
- Tachycardia
- Seizure

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Relative hypoglycaemia can occur in patients with chronically elevated glucose who develop symptoms at a higher BGL
- Patients with diabetes presenting with altered mental status should be presumed hypoglycaemic until proven otherwise
- Patients taking acarbose should be treated with pure glucose rather than sucrose-containing foods if oral therapy is used
- Glucagon may be less effective when glycogen stores are depleted
- Rebound or recurrent hypoglycaemia may occur, especially after insulin overdose, sulfonylurea exposure, or poor oral intake

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Ensure airway patency and provide assisted ventilations as clinically indicated
- Administer supplemental oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey with repeat BGL checks

**If conscious and able to swallow safely:**
- Administer oral glucose, sugary drink, or glucose gel
- Reassess GCS and BGL after 10 minutes
- Provide follow-up complex carbohydrate if needed

**If impaired conscious state:**
- Withhold oral intake if airway protection is impaired
- Administer intramuscular glucagon if within scope
- Consider advanced management
- Monitor closely and reassess conscious state and BGL

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain IV access if achievable
- Confirm IV patency before glucose administration
- Administer intravenous glucose, preferably 10% dextrose where available, titrating the dose to clinical effect
- Reassess GCS and BGL after treatment
- If BGL remains low or symptoms persist, repeat treatment and seek senior advice
- Consider benzodiazepines if seizing
- Consider cardiac monitoring

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Consider other causes of hypoglycaemia in patients without known diabetes, including sepsis, liver disease, alcohol, endocrine disease, starvation, and medication effects
